title: Abstract
content: This paper presents the synthesis of visible-light-harvesting photocatalyst, AgAgBrcarbon nanotubes (CNT) nanocomposites, by the photoreduction of AgBrCNT, which was independently prepared by the deposition-precipitation method.Nanocomposites with different carbon tubes lengths were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques.AgAgBr nanopaparticles were found to be anchored onto the surface of CNT.EIS measurements suggested that the longer CNT in AgAgBrCNT were more efficient in transporting charges than that of the shorter length CNT, which agreed with the observed trend of photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 under visible light ( 420 nm) (AgAgBrCNT-L AgAgBrCNT-M AgAgBrCNT-S).The study on the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 to methane, CO, methanol, and ethanol suggested that the reduction process favored under neutral and weak alkaline conditions.AgAgBrCNT can maintain the high stability in five repeated uses.

title: Experimental
content: Multi-walled carbon nanotubes were purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech.Port Co., Ltd. and were classified as CNT-S (CNT-1020 with 0.5 m length), CNT-M (CNT-1020 with 1-2 m length), and CNT-L (CNT-1020 with 5-15 m length).Details of CNTs are given in Table 1.The CNTs were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition from the CH 4 H 2 mixture at 700 C using Ni as a catalyst.The synthesized CNT were purified using the mixture of HNO 3 and H 2 SO 4 solutions, which decreased contents of metal catalyst and amorphous carbon.Carbon dioxide gas (99.99) was obtained from Foshan Ruike Gas Co. Ltd., China.Silver nitrate of analytical grade was purchased from Guangdong Guanhua Chemical Co. Ltd.China.Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, C 16 H 33 N (CH 3 ) 3 Br -; purity: 98) was obtained from Tianjing Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. in China.Other chemicals with analytical grade were obtained as reagents and were used without further purification.Deionized water was used throughout the study. The schematic diagram of synthesized AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites is presented in Fig. 2. AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites were prepared by the deposition-precipitation process in the presence of cation surfactant, CTAB, followed by photoreduction method according to the method reported in our previous work 22,23.An appropriate amount of CNT was oxidized by HNO 3 solution (HNO 3 :H 2 O 1:4, vv) for 1 h boiling, then washed by distilled water until the filtrate had neutral pH.The sample was added to 100 mL of CTAB aqueous solution with a concentration of 0.01 M (10 times above the CMC of CTAB 9.8 10 -4 M) 22, and the suspension was sonicated for 30 min, followed by magnetically stirring at room temperature for 12 h.Then 0.0012 mol of AgNO 3 in 2.3 mL of ammonia hydroxide (25 wt. NH 3 ) was rapidly added to the suspension.In this process, CTAB was adsorbed onto the surface of CNT at alkaline condition to limit the number of nucleation sites for the formation of AgBr aggregates; leading to well-dispersed AgBr anchored on CNT material.In addition, the amount of bromide ion from CTAB was more than sufficient to precipitate Ag from the added AgNO 3 in aqueous solution.The above solution was irradiated using a 150 W Xe arc lamp.The resulting precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water, dried at 75 C, and subsequently sintered in a muffle furnace at 400 C in the presence of N 2 for 3 h.In the control experiment, AgAgBr powders were prepared by a similar method without doping CNT. The prepared AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites powders were initially analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) method using a diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany) with radiation of Cu target (K , 1.54059A).Specific BET surface area was determined with an Autosorb-1-C ChemisorptionPhysisorption Analyzer (Quantachrome, USA) by nitrogen adsorption at 77.3 K. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a JSM-6330F field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 20 kV.The content of AgBr in the catalyst was determined by SEM-EDS-EBSD thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan).AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites powders were then examined by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR).X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was recorded with the AlK line at 150 W using an X-ray Photoelectron SpectroscopyESCA (ESCALAB 250).Zeta potential measurements were performed using a ZetaPALS analyzer (Brookhaven, USA). The EIS measurements were carried out using a potentiostatgalvanostat (Autolab, PGSTAT302N) coupled to a lock-in amplifier (PGSTAT 302N).The impedance data were collected as a function of frequency scanned from the highest (1000 Hz) to the lowest (0.001 Hz) using the "single-sine" (lock-in amplifier) method.The measurements were conducted in a conventional Htype electrochemical Pyrex cell with a standard three-electrode assembly.This assembly had a AgAgBrCNT working electrode, a Pt wire as a counter electrode (area, 0.5 cm 1.0 cm), and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode.To prepare AgAgBrCNT electrodes, ITO plates were first cleaned by sonication in a mixture of acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 10 min.ITO plates were coated with AgAgBrCNT slurry, containing 3 wt.poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) as a binder.The loading amount of the electrode was controlled to be 1 mg cm -2 .Finally, AgAgBrCNT-coated ITO plates were dried at 70 C for 180 min to evaporate the excess NMP; yielding AgAgBrCNT electrodes.A bias was controlled to 300 mV applied to the photoelectrochemical system.The initial pH in the solution was adjusted to 5.8 using 0.1 M KCl and 0.1 M NaOH.A 71LX 150 W middle-pressure Xenon arc lamp having a filter to remove light 420 nm wavelength was used as the visible light source. Photocatalytic reduction was carried out in a stainless steel vessel with valves for evacuation and gas feeding reported in our previous work (Fig. S1), in which an O-ring sealed glass window was placed at the top for allowing light irradiation.A 150 W Xe lamp (Shanghai Aojia Lighting Appliance Co. Ltd.) with UV cutoff filter (providing visible light with 420 nm) was used for irradiation.The intensities of visible light were measured as 25 mW cm -2 and 22.4 mW cm -2 at the irradiated surface of the reactor and at the bottom of the solution, respectively.In a typical batch, 0.5 g of prepared AgAgBrCNT powders were suspended in 100 ml of 0.2 M KHCO 3 solution in the vessel.Prior to the irradiation, pure CO 2 (99.99) was passed through the solution for 30 min to remove the oxygen, and then a flow was closed to maintain a pressure of 7.5 MPa inside the reactor.During the reaction, powders were continuously agitated to prevent the sedimentation of the catalyst by a magnetic stirrer, and the photocatalytic reaction was carried out for 5 h at room temperature.After illumination, small aliquots of the suspension were withdrawn by syringe and filtered through Millipore membranes.The filtrates were subjected to analysis.Reaction products in liquid phase were analyzed using a gas chromatograph (Agilent HP6890N) equipped with a flame ionization detector (FID) and a HP-5 capillary column (30 m 320 m 0.50 mm).Gas samples were collected with a gas-tight syringe through a septum and the products in gas phase were analyzed by GCMS Hewlett-Packard (HP) 6890 gas chromatography with a HP 5973 mass detector.A 60 m length 0.32 mm I.D 1.8 m film thickness HP-VOC column (Agilent Scientific, USA) was used.The oven was programmed as: the initial temperature of the column was 40 C hold for 2 min, followed by a ramp of 5 C min -1 to 120 C (1 min hold).

title: Results and discussion
content: The AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites were first examined by the XRD technique (Fig. 3).The crystalline diffraction peaks at 26.7 , 30.9, 44.3 , and 55.0 were identified as the (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (2 2 2), and (4 0 0) planes of hexagonal structure for the synthesized AgBr, respectively 24.The diffraction peaks at 26.2 and 42.1 could be assigned to the (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of hexagonal graphite structure with an interlayer spacing of 0.34 and 0.21 nm; indicating that CNT-S, CNT-M and CNT-L had the same structures.The peak of the (0 0 2) plane was of high symmetry of which the intensity of the peak increased with the tube length; suggesting a good crystalline dimension and a long-range order (i.e.CNT-L CNT-M CNT-S) 34.A weak peak at 38.1 was also seen in samples of AgAgBrCNT, which was assigned to Ag 0 metallic 35.The formation of Ag 0 on AgBr was therefore from the photoreduction of AgBrCNT. The results of nitrogen adsorption-desorption studies on CNT and AgAgBrCNT showed a type IV isotherm (Fig. 4).The specific surface areas of CNT-L and AgAgBrCNT-L, calculated from the linear region of the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) plot, were 93.53 and 53.45 m 2 g -1 , respectively.Similarly, the specific surface areas of CNT-S and AgAgBrCNT-S were 128.44 and 85.34 m 2 g -1 , and the specific surface areas of CNT-M and AgAgBrCNT-M were 113.40 and 76.02 m 2 g -1 .These specific surface areas of CNT and AgAgBrCNT clearly demonstrated that the presence of AgAgBr in the AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites decreased the surface area of CNT.The micropore volumes of CNT-L and AgAgBrCNT-L, calculated from the nitrogen adsorption isotherm by the Fig. 4. Nitrogen adsorptiondesorption isotherms and pore size distribution (inset) of CNT-L and AgAgBrCNT-L.Saito-Foley (SF) method, were 0.024 and 0.022 cm 3 g -1 , respectively; indicating that the deposition of AgAgBr on CNT also decreased the micropore volume of CNT.The cumulative pore volume of CNT and AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites, calculated by the density functional theory (DFT) method, were 0.481 and 0.516 cm 3 g -1 , respectively.The increased pore volume of AgAgBrCNT was ascribed to the increase in the mesopore volume of AgAgBr (inset Fig. 4).The AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with large cumulative pore volume may facilitate the adsorption of CO 2 , leading to the increased photocatalytic activities for the reduction of CO 2 . The SEM images of AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites, presented in Fig. 5a-c shows a network for all types of CNT on which the homogeneous distribution of small spherical particles of AgBr were anchored.This was a clear indication of the formation of AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites.EDS analysis of AgAgBr in all AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites demonstrated that crystals of AgAgBr were uniformly dispersed on CNT surfaces.The mass ratio of AgAgBr to CNT in the powder was obtained as 21.4 wt. AgAgBrCNT) (Fig. 5d).This ratio is similar to the theoretical calculated value of AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites (20.0 wt. AgAgBrCNT). Fig. 6 shows the TEM images of AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites.AgBr nanoparticles with a size of ca. 10 nm were deposited on the surface of CNT (Fig. 6a), although the aggregation of some AgBr nanoparticles also occurred.The structure information of nanocomposites was further obtained by carrying out HRTEM (Fig. 6b-c).The interplanar spacing (0.338 nm) corresponded to the (0 0 2) planes of CNT, while the interplanar spacing (0.288 nm) and (0.236 nm) corresponded to (2 0 0) planes of AgBr and (1 1 1) planes of metallic Ag, respectively.The results of HRTEM were further confirmed that the AgAgBr crystals were anchored onto the surfaces of CNT through the photoreduction of AgBrCNT.This direct contact between AgAgBr and CNT may be responsible for improving the charge transfer between the nanostructure interfaces. The peaks of Ag 3d, Br 3d and C 1s were observed in XPS spectra of AgAgBrCNT (Fig. 7).Two specific peaks of Ag 3d located at ca. 368 and 374 eV could be attributed to Ag 3d 52 and Ag 3d 32 binding energies, respectively 36.The XPS of Ag 3d 52 showed that the fitted peaks at 368.10 and 368.73 eV corresponded to Ag(I) and Ag(0), respectively, while the Ag 3d 32 peaks at 374.15 eV and 374.78 eV corresponded to Ag(I) and Ag(0), respectively.Fig. 7b shows the peaks of Br 3d 52 and Br 3d 32 with the binding energy of 68.25 eV and 69.41 eV, which could be assigned to Br(I).The high-resolution XPS of C 1s showed the fitted main peak at 284.77 eV, which could be ascribed to C C or C C bonds of CNT (Fig. 7c).The peaks at 285.80 eV, 287.60 eV, and 291.00 eV, (Fig. 7c), may be ascribed to O H bonds, C O or C O bands, COO bands, and - transition carbon, respectively 33.Overall, the XPS result suggests the coexistence of Ag 0 and AgBr on the surface of CNT.The calculated content ratio of Ag(0) to Ag(I) was wt.8.6 (the atomic rate of Ag and Br is 1.15). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements of AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different CNT tube lengths were conducted under dark and visible light conditions to understand a tube length-dependent electron transfer process in the photocatalytic conversion of CO 2 reduction.The impedance data were collected for nanocomposites electrodes in 0.1 M KCl liquid at pH 5.8 (Fig. 8a).It can be seen from Fig. 8a that only one arc appeared on the Nyquist plot of EIS for all electrodes under both dark and visible light conditions; suggesting a simple electrode process under our experimental conditions 37,38.In addition, the arc radius on EIS Nynquist plot of nanocomposites was of the order of AgAgBrCNT-L AgAgBrCNT-M AgAgBrCNT-S.Generally, the smaller the arc radius on the EIS Nynquist plot, the lower the charge transfer resistance 39.Thus, the EIS results demonstrated impedance order as AgAgBrCNT-L AgAgBrCNT-M AgAgBrCNT-S.The lowest impedance value for AgAgBrCNT-L could be related to the good physical properties of long-range order for longer CNT (see Fig. 3a-c).Significantly, all AgAgBrCNT electrodes had lower impedance under visible light irradiation than that under dark.Thus, the presence of visible light was favorable to the electron transport in the AgAgBrCNT system. The plots were fitted with the equivalent circuit (Fig. 8b), which was consisted of charge transfer resistance (R 2 ), Warburg impedance (W), capacitance (C), and resistance (R 1 ).Since the electrolyte and the electrode connection for all samples measurements were the same, therefore, R 1 directly demonstrated the photocatalyst substrate resistance.By fitting the plots of EIS, R 1 and R 2 for nanocomposites electrodes under dark and visible light irradiation were obtained.The fitted parameters are given in Table 2.The impedance of AgAgBrCNT-L in the charge transfer process was lower than those of AgAgBrCNT-S and AgAgBrCNT-M.It is evident that the decrease in charge transfer impedance with the increasing length of CNT.Overall, the electron accepting and transporting properties of CNT-L in AgAgBrCNT-L was Values of fitting parameters evaluated using equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 8b for AgAgBrCNT.Chi-square values (X 2 ) 10 -4 were obtained in all cases. R1 ( cm -2 ) R2 ( cm much faster than that of CNT-S in AgAgBrCNT-S and CNT-M in AgAgBrCNT-M, which could be beneficial to the suppression of charge recombination, thereby the highest rate in catalytic reduction of CO 2 for AgAgBrCNT-L would be achieved.Results of impedance measurements support the proposed scheme of Fig. 1 for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 . The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite was evaluated by studying the reduction of CO 2 in water under the visible light irradiation ( 420 nm) for 5 h.The results showed the photocatalytic products of methanol, ethanol, methane and CO were formed using AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites under visible-light irradiation (Fig. S2).These products were not detected under dark and without the presence of photocatalysts conditions; indicating that the presence of both the visible-light irradiation and the photocatalyst were indispensable for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 .The products formed can be described by reactions (3)- (6).The exited holes, produced by reaction (1), reacted with adsorbed water molecules on the catalyst surface and formed OH radicals and H (reaction (2)).Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons undergo local AgAgBr-to-CNT charge transfer and reacted with CO 2 to yield products of the reduction (reactions (3)-( 6)). The product yields using AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different CNT lengths were more than the products obtained using AgAgBr crystals (Fig. 9).This supports the hypothesis (see Fig. 1) that the electron acceptance nature of the CNT in the nanocomposites could suppress charge recombination.Significantly, the yields of the products were dependant on the length of the CNT and the order was AgAgBrCNT-L AgAgBrCNT-M AgAgBrCNT-S.The longer CNT tubes could transport the charge to a longer distance without undergoing significant loss during the travel 31.Additionally, the long-range order for longer CNT was also favorable to the charge transfer, which was also evident from the EIS measurements.The process of electron transfer was dependent on the charge transfer impedance of AgAgBrCNT.The longer CNT in AgAgBrCNT were capable of transporting charges more efficiently than the shorter length tubes in AgAgBrCNT.In this charge transport, not only the recombination of the hole and electron was minimized, but also the capturing of Ag could be avoided to some extent.Therefore, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites exhibited higher photocatalytic activity and stability than those of AgAgBr crystals.Fig. 10 shows the effect of pH on the photocatalytic products yield using AgAgBrCNT-L nanocomposite, which had the highest photocatalytic activity.The product yields of the reduction of CO 2 increased with the increase in pH up to pH 8.5, followed by decrease with further increase in the pH.The neutral and weak alkaline pH conditions were beneficial to the photo conversion of CO 2 to chemical fuels.This could be explained by the commulative effects of the following factors: (i) the OH -ions in aqueous solution may act as strong hole-scavengers to decrease the recombination of hole-electron pairs 40, hence facilitate the reduction of CO 2 .In contrast, H in aqueous solution may be involved in competing with CO 2 for electron (i.e.H e - H ), leading to the less yields of chemical fuels 41; (ii) CO 2 dissolves more in basic solution compared to pure water or acidic solution (i.e.CO 2 OH - HCO 3 -) 19, resulting in a higher yields of chemical fuels in the photoreduction process; and (iii) the neutral and weak alkaline pH conditions promote the adsorption of CO 2 onto catalyst.This was confirmed independently by measuring zeta potentials of AgAgBrCNT-L nanocomposites (pH pzp 6.0).At pH 6.0, the electrostatic forces between CO 2 and AgAgBrCNT-L would be present while repulsive forces likely to exist at pH 6.0.In the neutral and weak alkaline pH range, higher adsorption of CO 2 onto nanocomposites would give a relatively higher photocatalytic reduction activity (Fig. 10). Finally, the stability of the developed photocatalysts, AgAgBrCNT, was tested by performing repeated photocatalytic reductions of CO 2 in aqueous solution (Fig. 11).It can be seen that AgAgBrCNT-L retained its yield of methane, methanol, ethanol, and CO in five repeated uses at 28.45 2.22, 17.21 1.34, 2.94 0.23 and 7.11 0.56 mol g -1 h -1 , respectively.The total yields of the photocatalytic products on AgAgBrCNT-L after the five repeated uses remained about 85 of the first run.The results clearly indicate that the AgAgBrCNT nanocomposite is an effective and chemical-stable catalyst.Furthermore, the AgAgBrCNT-L nanocomposites were also examined by XRD and XPS measurements after their repeated used.The XRD results showed no significant changes in AgAgBrCNT-L nanocomposites after their use (Fig. 3d), which suggests that the AgAgBrCNT maintained stability for repeated visible-light irradiation time period.This is consistent with the previous reported results 25,28,35.The XPS results showed that ca.18 of AgBr was additionally converted to Ag 0 after five repeated uses (the atomic rate of silver and bromide is 1.33 after five repeated uses), which reveals that the surface chemical composition rarely change after the photocatalytic reduction reactions.AgBr nanoparticles were thus well-dispersed on CNT to maintain their stability and photocatalytic activity.This high stability of AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites is similar to our previous report of AgBrTiO 2 nanocomposites 23.The stability of AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites would be advantageous in performing the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 under visible light.

title: Conclusions
content: In this study, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different tube lengths were successfully prepared.The surface analysis of the nanocomposites showed the deposition of AgAgBr nanoparticles on CNT and this arrangement was able to promote the separation of charge in order to achieve efficient photoreduction of CO 2 under visible light irradiation.The charge separation increased with increase in length of CNT, which was confirmed by EIS measurements.Reduction of CO 2 resulted in methanol, CO, methanol, and ethanol and the yields of products depended on the length of CNT and the solution pH.The optimum pH range for efficient reduction of CO 2 was 7.0-8.5.Importantly, AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites were stable in the repeated uses for CO 2 reduction under visible light. Fig. 1.Schematic of photoinduced electron transfer in AgAgBrCNT under visible light. Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of synthesized AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites. Fig.3.XRD pattern of AgAgBrCNT-S (a), AgAgBrCNT-M (b), AgAgBrCNT-L (c) and AgAgBrCNT-L after repeated batch runs (d). Fig. 5. SEM images of AgAgBrCNT-S (a), AgAgBrCNT-M (b) and AgAgBrCNT-L (c), and EDS spectrum of AgAgBrCNT-L (d). Fig. 6.TEM image of AgAgBrCNT-L (a), and HRTEM images of AgAgBrCNT-L (b)-(c). Fig. 7. XPS peaks of (a) Ag 3d (b) Br 3d and (c) C 1s for AgAgBrCNT-L. Fig. 8. (a) Nyquist plots are shown here for AgAgBrCNT electrodes in dark and under visible light irradiation.Measurements were made at 300 mV in order to avoid the mass transport limited regime.The frequency range was 0.001-1000 Hz, with frequency decreasing from left to right along the x-axis, however very low frequency points over the used y-axis range are not shown here.(b) Shows the equivalent circuit used to model this system, including a component R1 series resistance, R2 the charge-transfer resistance, C double layer capacitance, and W Warburg impedance. Fig.9.Yield of photocatalytic products of AgAgBr and AgAgBrCNT nanocomposites with different CNT length under visible-light irradiation for 5 h in pH 8.5 aqueous media. Fig. 10.Effect of pH value on photocatalytic products yield of AgAgBrCNT-L under 5 h visible light. Fig. 11.Photocatalytic products yield on number of batch runs for AgAgBrCNT-L. Properties of CNTs.Surface area (A surf ) was calculated from the adsorption-desorption isotherm of N2 at 77 K by the multipoint BET method.Ash contents were measured by heating the CNTs at 900 C for 10 h.). a Provided by the supplier.b Outer diameter (OD) measured by TEM, n 100.c The authors wish to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 20877025, 21273085), National Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. S2011010001836, S2011010003416), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 09lgpy20), Science and Technology Research Project from Guangzhou City (No. 12A42091603, 2010Z2-C1009) and Ministry of Science and Technology of China (No.
